Intercultural Communication

Under the
Same Sun

Personal Encounter Cultural Perception
"A door isn't unfriendly just because it's shut."
Part I — Personal Encounter
I — The Sun Side

We start the hike in Imlil, the primary base camp for climbing Mount Toubkal, the highest peak in North Africa. We are waiting in the road for a local to bring water. An older man stands nearby, dressed in white, a member of the Berber tribe, indigenous inhabitants of this region. Two children pass, stop, take his hand, kiss it, say something in Arabic, and leave.

Nobody explained it. Nobody needed to. It was a ritual so embedded it required no thought. What I was watching was not just a greeting. It was a social contract, respect expressed through the body, passed between generations without words.

I recognised the logic. In Egypt, someone I had met only hours earlier invited us to eat with them. No hesitation, no negotiation. Just: come. We sat on the floor around a low table. I am tall, and my body had no idea what to do. The man next to me, without making a thing of it, showed me how to sit, then tore bread, dipped it into a green sauce, and gestured for me to do the same. It tasted like something I would not order again. But that was not the point. The point was that inclusion here was instinctive. A stranger did not need a reason to feed you. Hospitality was not an offer. It was a default.

In Cyprus, where I grew up, the same logic applies. Life happens outside. In the summer, which is most of the year, people eat on terraces, gather in squares, sit outside cafes for hours without needing a reason to stay. You do not walk past someone you know without stopping. You do not leave a table without checking everyone is fine. Strangers make conversation in queues. Neighbours know each other's business not through nosiness but through proximity, through a culture where private life and public life are not kept strictly apart. Warmth is not performed, it is assumed, and it is visible. I grew up thinking this was simply how people were. It took leaving to understand it was how we were.

☀ The Sun Side
High-context · Visible relational life Morocco High-context · Outdoor · Relational
Hospitality as default, not offer Egypt High-context · Outdoor · Relational
Where I learned what warmth looks like Cyprus High-context · Outdoor · Relational

Morocco, Egypt, Cyprus. The common thread was not just culture. It was sun. Outdoor life. Interaction that spilled into the street because the street was an extension of the home. The sun shaped the rhythm of daily life, who went outside, when, and for how long. It made social interaction visible. Research supports this pattern: studies suggest that higher sunlight exposure correlates with more collectivist, group-oriented social structures, where relational rituals become embedded in daily public life (Chiao & Blizinsky, 2010). The hand-kissing in Morocco, the floor meal in Egypt, these were not exceptional gestures. They were the natural output of a culture built around visible, outdoor, relational life.

And because that was all I had known, I had come to read it as the only valid form of warmth. So when I started travelling north, I carried that assumption with me. And it immediately started to fail.

II — Going North

In England, I made eye contact with a man passing on the high street. Brief, natural, the kind I had made a thousand times before. He stopped. "What are you looking at?"

My first interpretation was simple: unfriendly. I remember standing there replaying it, trying to work out what I had done wrong. Was it the eye contact? Was he having a bad day? Or was this just normal here? The confusion itself was revealing. Back home, that interaction would never have happened because eye contact between strangers carries a completely different meaning. It is an opening, not a provocation. I had walked into England with a set of social instincts that made perfect sense in Nicosia and meant something else entirely on an English high street. The discomfort I felt was not evidence that English people were unfriendly. It was evidence that I had entered a different system without realising it.

In Iceland, the contrast took a different shape. Walking through Reykjavik, two teenage girls approached us, made eye contact, came closer, and then at the last second turned and ran, laughing as they disappeared around a corner. They had clearly wanted to speak to us. But something stopped them. Not hostility. The opposite, in fact. What was missing was the social script that, back home, makes approaching a stranger feel unremarkable. In its absence, the interaction collapsed before it started.

In Poland, I tried to order pierogi at a restaurant counter. I said it in English. The woman responded in Polish. I didn't understand. She repeated herself, this time with an edge. I said, give me the best one. She made a face, tapped the card machine toward me, and the interaction was over. No warmth, but no cruelty either. Just a clear expectation, unmet, and a transaction that closed the moment it was no longer necessary.

England, Iceland, Poland. Less sun, more walls. The street was not a social space. It was a corridor.
❄ The Other Side
Eye contact as intrusion, not invitation England Low-context · Indoor · Transactional
Wanting to connect, no script to do it Iceland Low-context · Indoor · Reserved
An expectation unmet, then closed Poland Low-context · Indoor · Transactional
☀ High sunlight · outdoor · relational ❄ Low sunlight · indoor · bounded
Morocco Egypt Cyprus Poland England Iceland
III — The Shift

At first, I mapped this onto a simple contrast. Sunlight countries were warm. Non-sunlight countries were cold. The evidence seemed to line up. The kissed hand in Morocco, the floor meal in Egypt, the open door in Cyprus on one side. The confrontation in England, the retreat in Iceland, the silence in Poland on the other.

But that reading was too easy, and it was wrong.

What I had experienced across these countries had a name. Several, in fact.

Edward Hall (1976) distinguishes between high-context and low-context cultures. In high-context cultures, meaning is carried through nonverbal cues, shared rituals, and relational signals. Words are often unnecessary because the context does the work. Cyprus, Morocco, Egypt operate this way. The kissed hand, the torn bread, the open door, none of these required explanation. They were understood. In low-context cultures, interaction is explicit, bounded, and transactional. Meaning is stated, not implied. England, Poland, Iceland lean this way. The card machine tapped. The question asked. The interaction closed. Neither mode is warmer than the other. They are simply different systems, and I had been fluent in only one of them.

My assumption that visible expressiveness equals warmth also has a name. McCrae and Terracciano (2008) documented across 49 nations the widespread tendency to associate warmer climates with interpersonal warmth and sociability. I had applied this stereotype without realising it, converting outdoor visibility into a measure of human openness, and its absence into coldness. This is not an uncommon error. It is a documented one.

Underlying all of it was ethnocentrism, the tendency to use one's own culture as the invisible standard against which all others are measured (Bennett, 1986). I was not consciously judging. But I was unconsciously ranking. My framework was Cypriot, Mediterranean, shaped by sun and open doors. Every culture I entered was being filtered through that lens, and anything that didn't match was read as a deficit.

Sunlight, as Chiao and Blizinsky (2010) suggest, does play a contextual role. Higher UV exposure correlates with more collectivist social structures, where group-oriented rituals become embedded in public life. But context is not cause. Sun shapes where and how social life becomes visible. It does not determine whether warmth exists.

This framework reframes each encounter precisely. In England, I imported a high-context signal, open eye contact, into a low-context space where it carried no relational meaning and was instead read as a violation of public anonymity. In Iceland, the girls possessed the impulse to connect but lacked the social scaffolding that makes unsolicited interaction feel natural. In Poland, the exchange followed a transactional script where efficiency was the norm and my inability to meet its basic requirements was the friction. None of these were failures of warmth. They were failures of translation.

Behaviour follows cultural rules. Different cultures construct different rules. And when we encounter behaviour that doesn't match our own rules, our instinct is to read it as a failure rather than a difference. That instinct is the problem.

Sunlight shaped the context, not the character. It influenced where social life happened, how visible it was, what rhythms it followed. But it did not determine warmth. What it determined was form. And when I mistook form for content, I misread everyone I encountered.

While sunlight shapes social environments and warmth stereotypes (McCrae & Terracciano, 2008; Chiao & Blizinsky, 2010), my encounters reveal friendliness as culturally constructed behaviour, interpreted through culturally constructed lenses. Recognising the difference between form and content, between high-context rituals and low-context transactions, is what intercultural communication actually requires.

Part II — Cultural Representation
Part II — What Warmth Looks Like

We do not only learn culture through direct experience. We learn it before we arrive.

Music videos teach us what warmth looks like. They select particular behaviours, environments, and interactions, organise them into recognisable patterns, and repeat those patterns until they feel like fact. By the time we arrive somewhere new, we already carry an interpretive framework for reading it. Part I showed where that framework comes from. Part II asks what happens when it is applied at scale, before any encounter takes place.

This section examines two videos: Ελεύθερος Για Μια Ζωή by Konstantinos Argiros (2022) and Stolen Dance by Milky Chance (2013). Both express deep emotion. Both construct that emotion visually. But they do so in opposite ways, and those opposites map almost exactly onto the divide between high-context and low-context cultures.

IV — The Two Videos

In Ελεύθερος Για Μια Ζωή, the opening shot is telling. The performer sits in a living room holding a fish tank, enclosed, looking outward. The image carries the weight of the lyrics: the desire to travel, to get lost, to find himself. Confinement as the starting point. What follows is its opposite. As the chorus hits, the scene shifts to people dancing and jumping together, bodies in motion, arms raised. From there the video moves through beaches, houses, shared meals, outdoor gatherings. People are never incidental. They are always present, always part of the frame. The melody is built on a traditional Greek folk song, expansive and unbroken, and the visuals match it: each scene open, inhabited, bathed in light. The climax arrives with fireworks and people dancing on tables, collective joy at full volume. The arc of the video is a movement from solitude toward communion, from the fish tank to the table. Freedom, here, is not found alone. It is found in the presence of others.

In Stolen Dance, a single performer sits on a stool with a guitar. He does not move toward anyone. Behind him, scenes shift and blur, a lonely tree, fragments of landscape, occasional faces, but nothing coheres into a place or a relationship. The backgrounds are faded, sometimes in black and white, as if pulled from memory or the past rather than the present. At moments the colour shifts into something psychedelic, oversaturated, RGB-tinged, the visual language of disorientation, of a mind circling something it cannot reach. Some faces appear briefly and smile, but they make no contact and form no connection. The lyrics speak of absence and longing, "coldest winter for me, no sun is shining anymore." The occasional burst of fireworks punctuates the video, but even these feel distant, observed rather than shared. The structure never changes. The performer stays on his stool. The world behind him moves, blurs, and dissolves, but he remains alone inside it. Stolen Dance encodes exactly this logic, emotion present but entirely self-contained, requiring no external space to become legible.

V — Reading the Difference

The difference between the two videos is not the presence or absence of emotion. It is where that emotion lives.

Hall (1976) distinguishes high-context cultures, where meaning is carried by shared space, relationship, and relational flow, from low-context cultures, where meaning is made explicit through the individual. The contrast plays out exactly between these two videos. In Nicosia, meaning moves through the crowd. In Reykjavik, it rests with the performer alone. Neither grammar is warmer. Neither is shallower. They are different.

But they are not read that way. The climate-warmth association discussed in Part I (McCrae & Terracciano, 2008; Chiao & Blizinsky, 2010) is activated before the viewer forms a conscious response. Sunlight, open space, fluid movement: the viewer reads all of this as warmth, as openness, as friendliness. The Northern European video's restraint reads as distance, even when the feeling underneath is equivalent. The lyrics of Stolen Dance express profound longing, "I want you by my side so that I never feel alone again." The emotion is not shallow. It is simply invisible to anyone expecting it to announce itself through space and movement.

What the videos do, beyond activating a stereotype, is produce one. Stuart Hall (1997) argues that representation does not reflect cultural reality but constructs it, organising difference into visual codes that reduce complexity to a few repeatable traits and fix them until they feel natural. Both videos do exactly this. Ελεύθερος Για Μια Ζωή presents Greek life as perpetually sunlit, fluid, and communal, omitting the formality, reserve, and private grief that exist alongside its expressiveness. Stolen Dance presents Northern European interiority as cold and disconnected, omitting the warmth of friendships that, in many Northern European contexts, are built slowly and kept for life.

This simplification is not accidental. It is structural. Music videos are commercial products, designed to be globally legible and emotionally immediate. The archetype is not a failure of representation. It is the product itself. Complexity does not travel well. Nuance does not go viral. What travels is the recognisable image, the sun-drenched table, the solitary figure on a stool, stripped of everything that might slow the viewer down or complicate the feeling. The cultural stereotype is not a side effect of the format. It is what the format is built to produce.

Bennett (1986) describes the early stages of intercultural development as ethnocentric: the individual uses their own cultural framework as the invisible standard and reads difference as deficiency. His developmental model proposes that genuine intercultural competence requires moving through that stage, learning to recognise one's own framework as a framework rather than as neutral reality. Music videos make that movement harder. They do not present one cultural grammar alongside another as alternatives. They present one as legible and the other as affectively absent. The viewer is not invited to examine their own interpretive lens. They are confirmed in it.

This is what Part I could not show. A personal encounter is a one-to-one event, open to revision as it unfolds. A music video is pre-packaged meaning, delivered at scale, absorbed before any encounter takes place. By the time the viewer meets the culture the video represents, the interpretive framework is already installed. Ethnocentrism, in Bennett's (1986) sense, no longer has to be generated from personal bias. It has been pre-loaded.

The cultural stereotype is not a side effect of the format. It is what the format is built to produce.

The consequence is not simply that viewers hold inaccurate beliefs. It is that they apply the wrong interpretive framework with complete confidence. A viewer who has absorbed the warmth-sunlight association will not experience a reserved Northern European greeting as culturally different. They will experience it as unfriendly. The misreading feels like perception, not projection. There is nothing to correct because nothing feels wrong.

This is the mechanism that makes media-carried ethnocentrism more durable than the kind that emerges from direct contact. When I arrived in Germany and read restraint as coldness, I was at least in the presence of evidence that could eventually revise my reading. A music video viewer carries the framework without any encounter to test it against. The archetype circulates, is reinforced by other archetypes, and settles into assumption. By the time the actual encounter happens, the verdict is already in.

VI — Scale and Consequence

Warmth does not change across contexts. Its grammar does.

High-context relational flow and low-context individual containment are not opposite ends of a scale from warm to cold. They are alternate structures through which emotion finds expression. Sunlight makes the high-context version visible in public space. It spills into streets, into squares, into shared tables and open doors. The low-context version stays indoors, stays bounded, stays private. Climate shapes visibility. It does not shape depth.

The scale at which these representations circulate matters. Dyer (1993) argues that stereotypes gain their power not from any single image but from repetition: the same reduction confirmed across enough contexts that it stops feeling like a claim and starts feeling like a fact. Stolen Dance has been viewed nearly a billion times on YouTube. Ελεύθερος Για Μια Ζωή resonates across the Greek diaspora from Athens to Melbourne to Chicago, carrying its image of communal, sunlit belonging wherever it travels. These are not marginal cultural objects. They are templates, absorbed by enormous audiences, many of whom will carry their interpretive framework into real encounters with the cultures they represent. The reach of the representation is part of the argument. The more widely a simplified image circulates, the more it shapes expectation. The more it shapes expectation, the more confidently people misread what they find.

Music videos package these structures into recognisable, repeatable forms. They make one way of expressing warmth feel universal and another feel like its absence. Intercultural communication requires seeing past that. It requires knowing that the form is not the content. That a door is not unfriendly just because it is shut.

References

Bennett, M. J. (1986). A developmental approach to training for intercultural sensitivity. International Journal of Intercultural Relations, 10(2), 179–196.

Dyer, R. (1993). The matter of images: Essays on representations. Routledge.

Chiao, J. Y., & Blizinsky, K. D. (2010). Culture–gene coevolution of individualism–collectivism and the serotonin transporter gene. Proceedings of the Royal Society B, 277(1681), 529–537.

Hall, E. T. (1976). Beyond culture. Anchor Books.

Hall, S. (1997). Representation: Cultural representations and signifying practices. Sage.

McCrae, R. R., & Terracciano, A. (2008). The five-factor model and its correlates in individuals and cultures. In F. J. R. van de Vijver, D. A. van Hemert, & Y. H. Poortinga (Eds.), Multilevel analysis of individuals and cultures (pp. 249–283). Lawrence Erlbaum.

Argiros, K. (2022). Ελεύθερος [Music video]. Panik Platinum.

Milky Chance. (2013). Stolen Dance [Music video]. Lichtdicht Records.
